data.title: Homeland Security Democrats: Noem will be impeached if she is not fired, resigns
data.description:
House Homeland Security Committee Democrats said Tuesday that Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem is going to be impeached if she is not fired or steps out of her role.

“Kristi Noem has destroyed public confidence in her ability to lead [the Department of Homeland Security (DHS).] She lied about Alex Pretti,” the panel’s Democrats posted Tuesday on the social platform X.

“She has been rebuked by Republicans in Congress, by her own senior staffers, and even the President. She needs to be fired, resign, or she will be impeached,” they added.

The post comes after intense backlash toward the Trump administration over the fatal shootings of Alex Pretti and Renee Good in Minneapolis. Both were shot and killed by federal immigration officers.

Noem had said that Pretti “attacked” federal law enforcement and was “brandishing” a firearm, calling the incident an example of “domestic terrorism.” She made similar comments about Good earlier this month.

The House Democrats join Senate voices in calling for action on Noem.

Sen. Ruben Gallego (D-Ariz.) said she committed an “impeachable” offense by calling Pretti a “domestic terrorist.”

And centrist Democratic Sen. John Fetterman (Pa.) called on Trump her. “President Trump: I make a direct appeal to immediately fire Secretary Noem. Americans have died. She is betraying DHS’s core mission and trashing your border security legacy,” Fetterman said in a statement.

But President Trump on Tuesday, when asked if Noem will step down, responded, “No.”

Noem has agreed to testify in front of the Senate Judiciary Committee in a little over a month, testimony that was requested before the incidents in Minneapolis. 

Senate Homeland Security Committee Chair Rand Paul (R-Ky.) has also sent letters to the leaders of Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) to testify in front of the upper chamber’s Homeland Security Committee.

The Kentucky Republican told the leaders of President Trump’s immigration agencies that “Congress has an obligation to conduct oversight” of what he referred to as the “exceptional amount of funding” the Republican Congress has set aside to secure borders and enforce immigration law.

The Hill has reached out to DHS for comment.

data.title: Michigan Gov. Gretchen Whitmer mourns death of 'beloved father'
data.description:
LANSING, Mich. (WLNS)– Michigan Gov. Gretchen Whitmer announced on Tuesday that she is mourning the death of her father, Richard Whitmer, a behind-the-scenes power player in state government and health care who, she says, set her on a path to the governor’s office.

Richard Whitmer died Monday at age 85, according to the governor’s office. The cause of death was not immediately released.

“He was my best friend, most trusted counselor, and next-door neighbor until I moved into the Governor’s Residence,” Whitmer said in a statement. “I would not be where I am and who I am without my dad. Today, I’m sad, but I’m grateful too. I’m grateful for the time we got to spend together, for the things he taught me, and the stories I’ll carry with me forever.”

Born and raised in Michigan, Richard Whitmer was a lawyer by training who cut his teeth in state government. After graduating from Western Michigan University in 1963 and earning his law degree from the University of Michigan in 1965, he joined state service and rose quickly. He served in several roles in Lansing, including commerce secretary to Republican Gov. William Milliken, a top cabinet position that gave him a front-row seat to the state’s economic and political decisions in the 1970s.

He eventually left state government for the private sector, but he stayed in a role that kept him close to public policy. He joined Blue Cross Blue Shield of Michigan in 1977 as a senior vice president and general counsel. A decade later, he was elevated to president and chief executive officer at a time when the nonprofit insurer was in financial crisis and facing rocky relations with state officials and other stakeholders.

Blue Cross leaders now credit Whitmer with stabilizing the company, smoothing out the politics around health policy and rebuilding trust with labor and other partners. He retired from Blue Cross in 2006.

In public statements over the years, Gov. Gretchen Whitmer has talked about starting college at Michigan State University and planning to go into sports broadcasting. According to the governor, it was her father who suggested she consider public service and encouraged her to seek an internship at the Capitol, a pivot that eventually led her to law school, the Michigan Legislature and the governor’s office.

“He was the one who always saw my potential and believed in me, from the first speech I gave in fifth grade to my State of the State as governor,” Whitmer said. “He was always there, whether in the back of the classroom or in the front row of the House Chamber, cheering me on.”

Richard Whitmer is survived by his sister, his three children — Gretchen, Richard and Liz — and seven grandchildren.

data.title: Newsom launches review of TikTok over claims of suppressed Trump criticism
data.description:
California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) said Monday that he is launching a review of TikTok following allegations that the app’s new owners were suppressing content critical of President Trump.

Newsom’s office said it had received reports and “independently confirmed instances” of content being suppressed.

“It’s time to investigate,” the governor wrote in a post on X. “I am launching a review into whether TikTok is violating state law by censoring Trump-critical content.”

The probe comes as numerous users complained in recent days that TikTok was restricting certain videos and messages.

Several accused the app of suppressing videos about Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE). California state Sen. Scott Wiener (D), who is running for Congress in San Francisco, noted on X that a Monday TikTok post about ICE-related legislation “was sitting at zero views.”

This follows several days of tensions in Minneapolis, after a Border Patrol agent shot and killed 37-year-old Alex Pretti. It is the second time in a matter of weeks that immigration officers have fatally shot a U.S. citizen in the city.

Other TikTok users said they encountered error messages when sending messages containing the word “Epstein.” This comes amid a political fight between several lawmakers and the Trump administration over the release of files related to the convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

TikTok USDS Joint Venture, the platform’s new U.S. business, said late Monday that it was “continuing to resolve a major infrastructure issue triggered by a power outage at one of our U.S. data center partner sites.” Hundreds of thousands of Americans lost power over the weekend amid a winter storm.

“While the network has been recovered, the outage caused a cascading systems failure that we’ve been working to resolve together with our data center partner,” the company said in a statement.

“You may notice multiple bugs, slower load times, or timed-out requests, including when posting new content,” it added. “Creators may temporarily see ‘0’ views or likes on videos, and your earnings may look like they’re missing. This is a display error caused by server timeouts; your actual data and engagement are safe.”

It said Tuesday morning that it had made “significant progress” while noting users may still experience some technical issues. It also underscored that videos of the incident in Minnesota have been available since Saturday and that they do not have rules against sharing the name “Epstein” in direct messages.

TikTok announced last week that it had finalized deal to spin off its U.S. business, creating a new venture majority owned by U.S. investors. Oracle, Silver Lake and MGX, which each hold a 15 percent stake in the new venture, are managing investors.

The spin-off came in response to a 2024 law requiring TikTok’s China-based parent company ByteDance to divest from the app or face a ban on U.S. networks and app stores. Trump signed off on the deal last fall, which allows TikTok to continue operating in the U.S.

Oracle’s involvement has raised concerns for some, who have noted the company’s ties to Trump. The software firm, whose co-founder Larry Ellison is a key Trump ally, is in charge of retraining TikTok’s algorithm on U.S. user data.

data.title: Live updates: Trump says Noem will not step down amid mounting resignation calls
data.description:
The Senate heads back to Washington on Tuesday as a partial government shutdown looms. But Democrats are eyeing funding for Department of Homeland Security (DHS), after the weekend killing of a Minneapolis man by a Border Patrol (CBP) agent.

Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) has indicated he intends to move forward with a six-bill funding package, which includes DHS, despite Democrats’ threats to block the measure. Thune must confront whether he can rally his conference and the White House around a modified bill that separates DHS funding from the package to avoid another shutdown.

It also comes as President Trump is making adjustments to his signature initiatives in the wake of Alex Pretti’s death on Saturday. Border czar Tom Homan will arrive Tuesday in Minneapolis, replacing Border Patrol commander Greg Bovino.

A federal judge in Minnesota also demanded that acting Immigration and Customs Enforcement chief Todd Lyons make an appearance in court later this week, threatening the law enforcement officer with contempt proceedings.

The shooting ignited a firestorm on both sides of the aisle, with Democrats calling for Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem to be fired or impeached, and Republican lawmakers expressing grave concern and calling for hearings.

Trump on Tuesday, when asked if Noem will step down, responded, “No.”

He will later will head to Des Moines, Iowa, to deliver remarks.

Follow live below.

data.title: America's disaster costs are soaring —taxpayers can't keep paying
data.description:
Federal disaster spending has become one of Washington’s fastest-growing budget obligations. The next decade will test America’s ability to withstand stronger storms, rising temperatures and more frequent wildfires. Fortunately for policymakers and city planning officials, investments in pre-disaster mitigation are not only proven to protect communities but can save billions in taxpayer dollars.

Natural disasters don’t just hit the communities in harm’s way; whether they’re in the disaster zone or are thousands of miles across the country, they affect every American taxpayer. When natural disasters strike, federal dollars are used to assist states’ rebuilding efforts. In 2024 alone, the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration estimated that natural disasters caused over $182 billion in damage nationwide.

These impacts aren’t just felt by homeowners or businesses; they have even emerged as a growing national security concern. Many military housing companies face a wide range of weather-related risks, including hurricanes, wind and hailstorms, flooding, and wildfires. The Department of Defense continues to face billions of dollars in disaster risks to bases, training grounds and critical infrastructure that keep our country safe. 

The Federal Emergency Management Agency plays a pivotal role in helping states manage the escalating cost of recovery. This financial assistance for local recovery efforts is currently provided through its Disaster Relief Fund, a critical source of funding when major disasters inevitably deplete local and state budgets. As a result of intensified natural disasters, Congress has had to approve larger and more frequent emergency funding packages to keep FEMA’s relief fund afloat. 

There is, however, an opportunity to reduce federal disaster expenditure by taking a more proactive approach to disaster mitigation. 

Investments in pre-disaster mitigation have been demonstrably effective. According to the National Institute of Building Sciences, elevating infrastructure can save more than $12 for every $1 spent. During Hurricane Sandy, for example, investments in natural infrastructure like wetlands reduced damages by more than 22 percent in half of the areas directly affected by the storm. This proactive investment in pre-disaster mitigation is estimated to have saved $625 million in direct flood damage.

For American taxpayers, every year of inaction means higher recovery spending, greater infrastructure losses and more strain on federal budgets. 

As highlighted in the SmarterSafer Coalition’s recent white paper, Resiliency and Pre-Disaster Mitigation: Investments that Benefit American Taxpayers, “Investments in resiliency and pre-disaster mitigation are the best strategy to safeguard taxpayers’ dollars while offering strong protections to vulnerable local communities.”

The paper discusses the short and long-term value of measures like strengthening federal support for hazard mitigation programs. For example, a long-term reauthorization of the National Flood Insurance Program can facilitate funding for necessary mitigation measures in flood-prone areas, which could save taxpayers millions of dollars.

Disaster spending can also be curbed by ensuring that federally funded projects are built with future risk in mind, requiring up-to-date flood mapping and incentives for communities that have already invested in resilient infrastructure.

Additionally, public-private partnerships can help deploy proven resilience measures more widely, providing capital and innovation into projects at a scale the government alone cannot achieve. For example, an AI tool recently developed by Google in collaboration with the National Hurricane Center is already revolutionizing disaster prediction. Technological innovation is reshaping the possibilities for disaster prevention, and public-private partnerships can provide the means to harness it.

As disaster costs climb year after year, the vicious cycle of destruction and recovery is neither sustainable nor fiscally responsible.

Stronger mitigation and smarter planning won’t eliminate disasters but will make them far less costly for communities and taxpayers alike. When federal policy prioritizes resilience, states and cities are able to plan ahead, strengthen vulnerable neighborhoods, and avoid the repeated budget shocks that often accompany natural disasters.

As disasters become more frequent and intense, we must invest before the damage occurs rather than shoulder the high cost of recovery year over year. A mitigation-first approach provides a practical strategy for safeguarding lives and saving tax dollars nationwide.

Chris Brown is the executive director of the SmarterSafer Coalition, which brings together a wide range of environmental groups, consumer and tax advocates, insurance professionals and housing stakeholders in support of environmentally responsible, fiscally sound approaches to natural catastrophe policy that promote public safety.

data.title: Martha Stewart speaks out against immigration crackdown after text from granddaughter
data.description:
Martha Stewart has waded into the debate over the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown after receiving a not-so-subtle push from her granddaughter.

The award-winning TV personality shared an Instagram post on Monday featuring a text her granddaughter, Jude, sent her following the fatal weekend shooting of 37-year-old Alex Pretti in Minneapolis by a U.S. Border Patrol agent.

 “I’m not sure it’s excusable to not be speaking up right now,” Jude’s text said.

Stewart, 84, wrote that her 14-year-old granddaughter was “sensitive to what is going on in our country,” and the note had inspired her to speak out.

“I am disheartened and sad each and every day that we cannot demonstrate our sympathy for the beleaguered, that we are told immigrants, which most of us are or descended from are unwelcome, that we cannot show our frustration in peaceful demonstrations and that we can be attacked and even killed by Federal troops,” Stewart wrote to her nearly 3 million followers.

“Things must and have to change quickly and peacefully,” she added.

A growing number of celebrities are encouraging their fans to speak out following the killings of two U.S. citizens in Minneapolis by federal officers during Operation Metro Surge.

“Hacks” actress Hannah Einbinder urged her followers to participate in a national strike on Friday to protest Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), writing on Instagram, “withholding our labor and capital is our most effective leverage.”

Some, including singer Katy Perry, have also encouraged people to call their senators and ask them to vote against a bill funding the Department of Homeland Security (DHS).

Others, like singer Billie Eilish, have shared clips of alleged violent interactions between federal officers and individuals and questioned why more celebrities are not using their platforms.

“hey my fellow celebrities u gonna speak up? or,” Eilish wrote in a now-gone Instagram story.

Public outrage over the Trump administration’s immigration enforcement tactics was further inflamed by Pretti’s death, prompting bipartisan calls for an impartial investigation and growing pressure to pull federal law enforcement out of Minneapolis.

President Trump signaled on Monday that he would scale back operations in Minnesota following a “very good” phone call with Gov. Tim Walz (D), a sign the White House may be softening its stance in response to the political fallout.

data.title: Holocaust Museum: Walz's Anne Frank comparison 'deeply offensive'
data.description:
Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) is facing criticism from the U.S. Holocaust Museum after he compared Anne Frank hiding from the Nazis in Germany to the current experience of children in Minnesota amid Operation Metro Surge.

“Anne Frank was targeted and murdered solely because she was Jewish. Leaders making false equivalencies to her experience for political purposes is never acceptable,” the Washington, D.C.-based museum wrote on the social platform X.

“Despite tensions in Minneapolis, exploiting the Holocaust is deeply offensive, especially as antisemitism surges,” the post continued. It was shared on the eve of International Holocaust Remembrance Day, which commemorates the anniversary of the liberation of Auschwitz-Birkenau.

The museum’s rebuke came a day after Walz commented during a press conference on the fatal shooting of 37-year-old Alex Pretti by a Border Patrol agent, suggesting children were “hiding in their houses” because of Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) raids, urging President Trump to withdraw federal law enforcement from the state.

“We have got children hiding in their houses, afraid to go outside,” Walz said. “Many of us grew up reading that story of Anne Frank. Somebody’s gonna write that children’s story about Minnesota.”

The comparison to Frank, who died in the Bergen-Belsen concentration camp in 1945 at the age of 15, quickly sparked backlash online.  

Shabbos Kestenbaum, a Jewish-American activist who sued Harvard University in early 2024 over their alleged failure to protect students against antisemitism, called the Democratic governor “evil” in a post online.

“One million Jewish children were killed during the Holocaust. Illegal immigrants are offered thousands of dollars to take a free flight home,” Kestenbaum wrote.

It was not the first time Walz attempted to draw similarities between the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown and Nazi Germany.

During a commencement speech in May of 2025, Walz likened ICE agents to a “modern-day Gestapo,” referencing the brutal secret police force that enforced Nazi law and helped carry out the Holocaust.

“They’re in unmarked vans, wearing masks, being shipped off to foreign torture dungeons, no chance to mount a defense, not even a chance to kiss a loved one goodbye, just grabbed up by masked agents, shoved into those vans, and disappeared,” he said at the time.

Celebrities, including author Stephen King and musician Bruce Springsteen, have also drawn parallels between the two.

“The United States, the ideals and the values for which it stood for the past 250 years, is being tested as it has never been in modern times,” Springsteen said during a recent concert in New Jersey, slamming federal law enforcement for using “Gestapo tactics.”

Scholars have previously argued that using the Holocaust as an analogy in any political or ideological sense can be dangerous because it oversimplifies a complex historical situation.  

“Careless Holocaust analogies may demonize, demean, and intimidate their targets. But there is a cost for all of us because they distract from the real issues challenging our society, because they shut down productive, thoughtful discourse,” Edna Friedberg, a historian at the Holocaust Museum, wrote in 2018.

“At a time when our country needs dialogue more than ever, it is especially dangerous to exploit the memory of the Holocaust as a rhetorical cudgel,” Friedberg continued. “We owe the survivors more than that. And we owe ourselves more than that.”

data.title: DHS stresses ICE will be under Italian authority at Olympics
data.description:
The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) said on Tuesday that U.S. immigration officers will remain under the purview of Italian officials at the upcoming winter Olympic games. 

Officers with Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), which is a branch of DHS, will assist with security operations at Italy’s Milano-Cortina Winter Olympic Games. The competitions begin on Feb. 6 and run through Feb. 22. 

The department responded to the news on the social platform X on Tuesday. 

“Obviously, ICE does not conduct immigration enforcement operations in foreign countries,” reads a post on DHS’s account. “At the Olympics, ICE’s Homeland Security Investigations is supporting the U.S. Department of State’s Diplomatic Security Service and host nation to vet and mitigate risks from transnational criminal organizations. All security operations remain under Italian authority.”

ICE’s involvement in the games has been met with criticism from some Italian officials, who cite U.S. law enforcement’s role in killing two people in Minnesota over the last month. Milan’s mayor told local media that ICE is “not welcome in Milan, without a doubt.”

Giuseppe Conte, Italy’s former prime minister and longtime critic of right-wing Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni, condemned “street violence and killings” at the hands of U.S. immigration agents, saying, “we cannot allow” ICE at the Olympics. 

“We cannot allow this,” Conte wrote in an X post translated from Italian. “After our protests, our Government had tried to downplay it, but these latest statements speak clearly about ICE’s determination to come and ensure ‘security’ in Italy as well. 

“Enough with the bows and scrapes—let’s set our own boundaries too,” he continued. “The Meloni Government must make clear decisions.”

Italian Interior Minister Matteo Piantedosi has said he doesn’t “see what the problem would be” if the U.S. officers helped to secure the games, The Associated Press reported. Piantedosi is reportedly meeting with the U.S. ambassador to Italy on Tuesday to talk about ICE’s role at the games.

data.title: Who are the 'domestic terrorists' in Minneapolis? Certainly not Pretti and Good.
data.description:
The events of 9/11 changed the nation and the world in numerous ways. Airport security was transformed after a group of 19 men hijacked four airplanes and used them as weapons to destroy the Twin Towers in New York City and inflict damage to the Pentagon. It led to the creation of the Department of Homeland Security, whose mission is “to secure the nation from the many threats we face.”  

Sept. 11, 2001, made the terms “terrorist” and “terrorism” part of everyday speech.

Today, the term “domestic terrorism” is being widely used by the Trump administration. His presidential memorandum, “Countering Domestic Terrorism and Organized Political Violence,” describes “domestic terrorists [as] wag[ing] a violent assault against democratic institutions, constitutional rights, and fundamental American liberties.” 

Domestic terrorism is defined by the Federal Bureau of Investigation as “violent, criminal acts committed by individuals and/or groups to further ideological goals stemming from domestic influences, such as those of a political, religious, social, racial, or environmental nature.” 

In Minneapolis, Immigration and Customs Enforcement officers are patrolling the area in search of “dangerous criminal illegal aliens … including murderers, rapists, gang members, and perpetrators of fraud.” In its Jan. 19 press release, Homeland Security highlighted 13 such individuals, with descriptions of their crimes. The department also noted that during the six weeks of operations to date, it had arrested “3,000 criminal illegal aliens including vicious murderers, rapists, child pedophiles, and incredibly dangerous individuals.” A complete list of such individuals and their crimes has not been released. 

So how much longer will such operations continue if all illegal aliens are to be arrested? To estimate this number, there are around 390,000 immigrants in the Minneapolis-St. Paul area (as of 2023) and around 164,000 non-citizens who may be at risk of deportation.

Given that Homeland Security claims to be searching for criminals in this group, and that around 8 percent of the adult population commits felonies, that means that around 13,000 people are their target population. However, studies have found that the felony rate among undocumented immigrants is around one-half the rate for U.S.-born citizens, bringing this number down even further.

At their current pace, these operations in Minneapolis should be completed within 10 weeks. If some of the non-citizens were in the process of becoming legalized, the time frame could be even shorter. 

However, the price paid by the residents of Minneapolis to date includes the deaths of Renee Good and Alex Pretti at the hands Homeland Security officers. Both were shot as legal protesters of immigration enforcement — one by an ICE officer, one by a Border Patrol agent. Protest is something the U.S. Constitution gives them the legal right to do. 

The secretary of Homeland Security immediately labelled Good as a domestic terrorist and Pretti’s actions as acts of domestic terrorism, simply because they opposed how immigration law was being enforced in their community. Their interactions with immigration officers and agents were captured by eye witnesses and on multiple videos, providing a clear picture of their interactions and what transpired.   

Given the president’s statement on domestic terrorism on Sept. 25, any groups or individuals that wage an assault on constitutional rights are domestic terrorists. By this definition, when federal officers attack or kill citizens engaged in constitutionally protected public protests, are the feds acting as domestic terrorists? Partisan spin is only creating greater divides and making the situation worse. 

What is most disturbing about these immigration enforcement operations is not what they are doing, but how they are going about doing it. Secretary Kristi Noem’s assertions that the operations are needed due to Gov. Tim Walz (D) and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey not protecting their own people are symptomatic of an operation smeared in politics. If the operation is truly necessary, why even mention the governor? Moreover, how are these operations protecting U.S. citizens in Minneapolis if they are killing U.S. citizens in Minneapolis?

Minneapolis did not experience the current social unrest until the Department of Homeland Security began using these tactics in the city. If a foreign terrorist group were to enter the city and created the same level of disruption, the FBI, local police and the Minnesota National Guard would be called up to bring stability and calm to the area. If foreign terrorists were to kill local people, investigations would be undertaken and criminal charges would be issued.

What immigration officers are doing, even with their mission supported by the administration, is no different than what a foreign terrorist operation would execute. 

It is time to refocus operations in Minneapolis for their intended purpose — not as a gestapo-like police force of the administration. 

Sheldon H. Jacobson, Ph.D., is a computer science professor in the Grainger College of Engineering at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign. As a data scientist, he uses his expertise in risk-based analytics to address issues impacting public policy.

data.title: Biden:  Minnesotans 'have suffered enough at the hands of this Administration'
data.description:
Former President Biden on Tuesday praised Minnesotans protesting federal immigration enforcement operations in the wake of two recent shootings.

“What has unfolded in Minneapolis this past month betrays our most basic values as Americans,” Biden wrote in a statement on the social platform X. “We are not a nation that guns down our citizens in the street. We are not a nation that allows our citizens to be brutalized for exercising their constitutional rights. We are not a nation that tramples the 4th Amendment and tolerates our neighbors being terrorized.”

He praised Minnesotans for helping each other “in unimaginable circumstances, speaking out against injustice when they see it, and holding our government accountable to the people.”

“Minnesotans have reminded us all what it is to be American, and they have suffered enough at the hands of this Administration,” Biden continued. “Violence and terror have no place in the United States of America, especially when it’s our own government targeting American citizens. No single person can destroy what America stands for and believes in, not even a President, if we — all of America — stand up and speak out. We know who we are.”

The former president called on “full, fair, and transparent investigations into the deaths of” Renee Good and Alex Pretti. An Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer shot and killed Good earlier this month after administration officials claimed she attempted to hit the officer with her SUV, a claim that caused uproar and fueled protests. Pretti died after a fatal shooting on Saturday involving U.S. Border Patrol officers. Authorities said he was killed for approaching officers with a handgun, but video footage shows a phone in Pretti’s hand.

“Jill and I are sending strength to the families and communities who love Alex Pretti and Renee Good as we all mourn their senseless deaths,” Biden said, referring to former first lady Jill Biden.

Biden’s call to Minnesotans comes days after former President Obama and former first lady Michelle Obama called Pretti’s death “a wake-up call to every American, regardless of party, that many of our core values as a nation are increasingly under assault.”

Democratic and some Republican lawmakers have called for an impartial investigation into Pretti’s death. Sen. Bill Cassidy (R-La.) warned on X that the credibility of ICE and the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) “are at stake.”

“Any administration official who rushes to judgment and tries to shut down an investigation before it begins are doing an incredible disservice to the nation and to President Trump’s legacy,” Sen. Thom Tillis (R-S.C.), an outspoken critic of the president, said on X.

On Monday, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt that DHS and the FBI are investigating the shooting, while U.S. Customs and Border Protection are investigating the incident as part of an internal review.
